agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management — specifically the cancellation policy governing when and whether a placed order may be cancelled before fulfillment. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern this behavior.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's committed request to purchase goods, subject to lifecycle state transitions."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing the commitment to fulfill it."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; represents a fulfillment milestone after which cancellation is no longer possible per Product."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay; per Finance, its creation is irreversible and prevents cancellation."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating a confirmed order; per Operations, this is the moment invoicing occurs to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from Product defining the cancellation window. A VP could change the cancellation cutoff point.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should be permitted (per Product)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Reversing an invoice has audit, tax, and compliance implications. If violated, financial records are corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, any attempt to cancel MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. The timing could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment instead) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is immediately created for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer attempts to cancel an order 1 second after placing it.","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: PM says yes (not yet shipped), Finance says no (already invoiced per Ops policy). Cannot define correct behavior."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but shipment is delayed for days.","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: The entire window between placement and shipment is simultaneously cancellable (PM) and non-cancellable (Finance + Ops)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Even if partial cancellation were considered, the invoice contradiction blocks it."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we follow PM and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance's books are corrupted and audit/tax compliance is at risk. If we follow Finance and block all cancellation, customer satisfaction drops and refund/support costs rise.","operational":"The operations team's decision to invoice at placement (to lock pricing) is the root cause of the collision. Changing invoice timing would resolve the contradiction but may introduce pricing risk.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If cancellation is blocked immediately after placement, it will generate support tickets, complaints, and potential churn.","risk":"Implementing any interpretation without resolving this contradiction risks either financial integrity violations or a broken customer experience. Legal/compliance exposure if invoiced orders are reversed without proper credit note workflows.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance rule wins; poor experience and potential churn."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Financial record integrity at risk if PM rule wins and invoiced orders are cancelled without proper reversal process."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of angry customers if cancellation is blocked, or complex manual processes if workarounds are needed."},{"group":"Operations","impact":"Their invoicing-at-placement policy is the fulcrum. Any resolution likely requires them to change process or accept new complexity."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Invoice reversal has tax and regulatory implications that no stakeholder has addressed."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholders have provided requirements that form a logical contradiction. BR-1 (cancel before ship) and BR-2 (cannot cancel if invoiced) and BR-3 (invoiced at placement) combine to mean: orders are cancellable before shipment AND orders are never cancellable after placement. Since placement precedes shipment, the cancellable window is simultaneously open and closed. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — it is a logical impossibility","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is correctly classified as an invariant — financial integrity","The three rules as stated cannot all be true simultaneously"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's rule should yield — this is a business decision, not a technical one","Whether the finance team would accept a credit note workflow as a resolution","Whether operations would accept moving invoice timing to shipment"],"assumptions":["Invoice in this context means a formal financial document with audit/tax implications, not a soft hold or price quote","Shipment always occurs strictly after placement (no pre-shipped or dropship edge cases)","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal process that Finance omitted from their constraint"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. PM says cancel before shipment. Finance says cannot cancel after invoicing. Operations says invoicing happens at placement. Therefore: orders are cancellable in the window [placement, shipment) AND non-cancellable in the window [placement, ∞). The intersection makes cancellation both allowed and forbidden for every placed, unshipped order.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Move invoice timing from placement to shipment. Orders become cancellable before shipment (satisfies PM), and invoicing still prevents post-invoice cancellation (satisfies Finance). Operations loses price-lock-at-placement.","B: Introduce a credit note / invoice reversal workflow. Orders can be cancelled before shipment, but cancellation triggers a credit note against the invoice rather than deleting it. Finance's audit trail is preserved. Requires Finance to accept that 'irreversible' means 'reversible via formal credit process.'","C: Introduce a two-phase model: a soft reservation at placement (locks pricing) and formal invoicing at shipment. Cancellation is allowed during the reservation phase. Satisfies all three but adds process complexity.","D: Accept Finance's constraint as absolute. Orders cannot be cancelled after placement. PM must revise the cancellation policy."],"recommendation":"Option C (two-phase: reservation + invoice) is the most domain-faithful resolution. It preserves price-lock semantics for Operations, maintains invoice irreversibility for Finance, and gives customers a cancellation window for Product. However, this is a business decision that requires all three stakeholders in a room. Escalating for resolution."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are logically contradictory and cannot be implemented as-is.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3 are mutually exclusive. Implementing any two violates the third."}]}
